title: Storage Throughput Guide
summary: Understand how IOPS, average I/O size, read/write mix, and overhead convert random activity into sustained MB/s demand.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates storage throughput demand from operation count, I/O size, read percentage, write percentage, and overhead.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when a workload has enough I/O volume that MB/s, storage path bandwidth, controller limits, or transfer behavior may become the limiter.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible Storage Throughput Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects sustained throughput pressure.

items.label: IOPS
items.description: The number of storage operations per second to convert into throughput demand.
items.whyItMatters: IOPS becomes MB/s once each operation has a size. High IOPS with larger blocks can create substantial bandwidth demand.
items.commonMistake: Checking IOPS alone and forgetting the storage path also has a throughput limit.

items.label: Avg I/O Size (KB)
items.description: The average size of each storage operation in kilobytes.
items.whyItMatters: Larger I/O sizes turn the same IOPS count into more MB/s.
items.commonMistake: Comparing IOPS numbers without matching block size.

items.label: Read %
items.description: The percentage of operations that are reads.
items.whyItMatters: Read-heavy and write-heavy workloads may stress different parts of the storage stack.
items.commonMistake: Treating all throughput as identical regardless of direction.

items.label: Write %
items.description: The percentage of operations that are writes.
items.whyItMatters: Write throughput can be constrained by parity, cache, media endurance, or sync behavior.
items.commonMistake: Trusting peak write specs instead of sustained write behavior.

items.label: Overhead (%)
items.description: Additional throughput load from protocol overhead, filesystem behavior, encryption, parity, metadata, or inefficiency.
items.whyItMatters: Overhead reduces the usable throughput available to the actual workload.
items.commonMistake: Assuming theoretical storage or network speed equals usable application throughput.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the throughput estimate appears manageable. Watch means block size, overhead, or storage path capacity should be reviewed. Risk means the storage path may not sustain the required MB/s even if IOPS looked acceptable.
bullets: Final throughput is the practical MB/s estimate after overhead.
Read/write split helps identify direction-specific pressure.
Throughput and IOPS should be evaluated together before sizing storage hardware.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: Storage Throughput follows Storage IOPS because the same I/O profile can create a very different bandwidth requirement depending on operation size and overhead.
bullets: Use IOPS as the upstream demand driver when available.
Carry the storage performance story into VM Density and backup planning.
Do not assume passing IOPS means the storage path is complete.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
